{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:

Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.

Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.

revision.text:
{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District aqqears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki suqqorts the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures suqqlied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shiqqing routes suqqorted economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures suqqlied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shiqqing routes suqqorted economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District aqqears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki suqqorts the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures suqqlied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shiqqing routes suqqorted economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Koma District]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:巨摩郡]]

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District aqqears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki suqqorts the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures suqqlied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shiqqing routes suqqorted economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki su11orts the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures su11lied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shi11ing routes su11orted economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District a11ears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki su11orts the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures su11lied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shi11ing routes su11orted economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appear[[Category:pp to pp]]s in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appear[[Category:pp to pp]]s in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appear[[Category:pp to pp]]s in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=draft}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=draft}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡|12=draft}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳|12=draft}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=draft}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=draft}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=draft}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄|12=draft}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=draft}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=draft}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=draft}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=draft}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|12=draft}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧|12=draft}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=draft}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=draft}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡|12=draft}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳|12=draft}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=draft}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=draft}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=draft}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄|12=draft}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=draft}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=draft}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=draft}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=draft}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|12=draft}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧|12=draft}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}
{{draft categories|
}}

[[sn:Koma District]] [[ja:巨摩郡]]
