revision.comment: 79 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:巨摩郡 up to rev 100203449
revision.text:
{{otheruses||漫画『バリバリ伝説』の登場人物|バリバリ伝説#第1部より登場}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|令制国一覧|東山道|甲斐国}} * {{Pathnav|日本|中部地方|山梨県}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|山梨県巨摩郡の範囲]] '''巨摩郡'''（こまぐん）は、[[山梨県]]（[[甲斐国]]）にあった[[郡]]。
== 郡域 == 消滅時の郡域は下記の区域にあたるが、行政区画として画定されたものではない。
* [[韮崎市]] * [[南アルプス市]] * [[北杜市]] * [[甲斐市]] * [[甲府市]]の一部（概ね[[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]以西および黒平町） * [[中央市]]の一部（[[笛吹川]]以北） * [[中巨摩郡]] * [[南巨摩郡]]の一部（[[富士川]]以西）
== 概要 == [[古代]]においては、'''巨麻郡'''とも表記され、中世以降に「巨摩」表記へ移行する。近世まで甲斐国は4郡で構成されていたが、その中で面積は最大。大まかにいうと甲斐国を東西に3分割した際の西部にあたる[[甲府盆地]]と南アルプス地域に相当する南北に長く広大な郡域で、のちの[[北巨摩郡]]、[[中巨摩郡]]の荒川以西、および[[南巨摩郡]]のうち富士川以西の地域に相当。
北部と北西部を[[信濃国]][[佐久郡]]、[[諏訪郡]]、[[伊那郡]]と接し、東は[[山梨郡]]、[[八代郡 (甲斐国)|八代郡]]と接し、西部と南部は[[駿河国]][[安倍郡]]、[[庵原郡]]と接する。北には[[八ヶ岳]]や[[茅ヶ岳]]、西には[[甲斐駒ヶ岳]]や[[北岳]]などがあり、郡中を釜無川が南流し、[[御勅使川]]や諸河川を合流して[[富士川]]にいたる。
=== 郡名の由来 === 初見史料は『[[正倉院文書]]』[[天平宝字]]5年（[[761年]]）12月「甲斐国司解」に見られる「巨麻郡栗原郷」。郡名の由来は二つの説があり、[[江戸時代]]に編纂された『[[甲斐国志]]』など近世の地誌類によれば、古代に多くの御[[牧 (古代)|牧]]が多く存在したことから、良馬（駒）を産出した地である事にちなみ「駒」という言葉が由来となったという説と、668年の[[朝鮮半島]]での[[高句麗]]滅亡以後、『[[続日本紀]]』霊亀2年（716年）5月16日条に甲斐はじめ関東や中部の7カ国へ高句麗からの[[渡来人|帰化人]]が渡っている記事が見られることから、[[高句麗]]からの帰化人にちなみ「高麗」という言葉が由来となったという説があった。[[近代]]までは前者の「駒説」が有力であったが、[[関晃]]による[[上代特殊仮名遣]]の研究により、「駒」のコは甲類、「高麗」のコは乙類で、乙類の「巨」に一致する「高麗説」が妥当であり、高句麗系渡来氏族大狛連（狛造）の本貫地である[[河内国]][[大県郡]]巨麻郷や[[若江郡]]巨麻郷に「巨麻」が当てられている事から、巨麻は高句麗（高麗）を意味すると考えられている<ref> {{Cite book|和書 |chapter=甲斐の帰化人 |title=甲斐史学 |volume=1号 |author=関晃|authorlink=関晃 |date=1957年 |year=1957 }}収録 - {{Cite book|和書 |title=関晃著作集 |volume=第5巻 |editor=関晃著作集編集委員会 |date=1997年2月 |year=1997 |publisher=[[吉川弘文館]] |id=ISBN 4-642-02305-4 }}</ref>。しかしながら「高」を甲類のコとする別の研究成果もあるため<ref> {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.techxel.jp/wp/wp-content/uploads/files/140107Manyogana.pdf |title=改訂新版・世界大百科事典「万葉仮名」の項（ジャパンナレッジ） |publisher=[[平凡社]]刊 |date=2007年 |year=2007 |accessdate=2018-10-13 }}</ref> <ref> {{Cite web|和書|url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=375 |title=万葉仮名甲類乙類音韻の研究 |author=[[田中健彦]]著 |date=2014年 |year=2014 |accessdate=2018-10-13 }}</ref>、万葉仮名の読みの分類から、巨麻と高麗と結びつける妥当性には諸説が存在する。
== 立評と古代の巨摩郡 == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|3頭分の馬歯が出土した甲斐市志田のお舟石古墳]] 『[[和名類聚抄]]』では等力、速見、栗原、青沼、真衣、大井、市川、川合、余部の9郷が見られる。このうち等力と栗原は[[飛び地]]であった可能性が指摘され、のちに青沼郷とともに山梨郡へ編入されている。また、市川郷の一部も八代郡へ編入される。古代甲斐国では[[甲斐の黒駒]]伝承に象徴される馬産が盛んな地域で、特に巨摩郡には[[真衣野牧]]・[[穂坂牧]]・[[柏前牧]]の三御牧が設置され、『[[延喜式]]』によれば朝廷に駒を貢進していたという。
巨麻郡へは[[朝鮮半島]]から[[高句麗]]遺民が遷置されたと考えられており、渡来人の墓制である[[積石塚]]が分布する。それ以前にも巨麻郡地域や甲斐国内へ定住していた[[渡来人]]集団の存在を示す形跡が残されている。甲府盆地の北縁では甲府市横根町・桜井町に分布する[[横根・桜井塚古墳群]]や、笛吹市春日居町の[[春日居古墳群]]など、[[甲府市]]羽黒町・湯村から横根町、笛吹市春日居町・石和町の山裾地域にかけて積石塚が分布している。中でも笛吹市石和町の[[大蔵経寺山古墳群]]の5世紀にまで遡る積石塚であると考えられている。
甲斐国と同じく積石塚が分布し、渡来人が立評に関わっているとされる[[信濃国]]北部の[[高井郡]]でも同様の傾向が見られる。高井郡でも古代には[[馬]]産が行われており、甲斐の巨摩郡でも積石塚の分布域と重なる盆地北縁の甲府市塩部の[[塩部遺跡]]や、盆地南部・[[曽根丘陵]]の甲府市下向山の[[東山北遺跡]]の[[方形周溝墓]]、[[甲斐市]][[志田 (甲斐市)|志田]]のお舟石古墳などで4世紀後半から5世紀初頭の[[馬歯]]や[[馬具]]が出土している<ref>村石「甲斐の馬生産の起源」『動物考古学』</ref>。信濃の高井郡や[[伊那谷]]も古墳時代からの馬の産地であり、甲斐・信濃の地域間での交流が指摘されている<ref>[[大塚初重]]「積石塚古墳と古代馬」『東国の古墳と大和政権』</ref>。
巨摩郡のうち積石塚が分布し古代において定住痕跡が見られる中核地域では、古代以前の[[縄文時代]]から[[弥生時代]]の遺跡が希薄な地域で、それまで未開発であった地域に対する立郡事情にも渡来人の関わりが考察されている。[[磯貝正義]]は栗原郷・等々力郷が飛び地であることに着目し、山梨郡の有力豪族が牧場経営などを目的に渡来人を利用して立郡したとする説を提唱している<ref>磯貝正義「郡の成立甲斐国巨麻郡の場合」『郡司及び采女制度の研究』</ref>。
一方、末木健は考古学的見地から、[[天狗沢瓦窯跡]]（甲斐市天狗沢）から出土した[[古代瓦]]に高句麗系軒丸瓦が発見されたことに着目し、寺院建立のために渡来系工人集団が畿内政権の支援を得て立郡した可能性を指摘している<ref>末木健「甲斐国巨麻郡の成立と展開」「甲斐仏教文化の成立」『山梨県立考古博物館・県埋蔵文化センター研究紀要』</ref>。
その後、巨摩郡における渡来人の足跡は途絶えるが、『[[続日本紀]]』に拠れば霊亀2年（[[716年]]）に甲斐を含む東国の高麗人を武蔵国へ移し高麗郡が置かれたとあり、中核集団が移住したとも考えられている。 <!-- === 式内社 === 『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]に記される郡内の[[式内社]]。 {{式内社一覧/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''巨摩郡''' |- ||||||||||||||| |} -->
== 中世以降 == 巨摩郡における[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]の初見は、『[[仁和寺文書]]』法勝院領目録の[[安和]]二年（[[969年]]）7月8日に記載される[[市河荘]]で、市河荘の立荘は甲斐国において早く、[[荘園公領制]]確立以前の10世紀に成立した荘園と評価されている。
中世には[[甘利荘]]、逸見、[[加々美荘]]、大井、[[布施荘]]、奈古、鎌田、大八幡、山小笠原、原小笠原、小井川、鷹津名、[[志摩荘]]などの諸庄が見られ、ほか小笠原、逸見、武川、八田、飯野などの牧や、藤井、杣などの[[保]]も成立する。平安時代後期には[[常陸国]]から流罪となった源義清・清光親子が市河荘に土着し、さらに八ヶ岳山麓の逸見牧をはじめその子孫は甲府盆地各地の荘園で進出し、[[甲斐源氏]]の一族となる。甲斐源氏は棟梁の[[武田氏|甲斐武田氏]]をはじめ、[[逸見氏]]、[[甲斐一条氏|一条氏]]、[[甘利氏]]、[[加賀美氏]]、[[小笠原氏]]、[[南部氏]]、[[奈古氏]]の氏族を分出した。このうち巨摩郡では棟梁の武田氏のほか、西郡一帯には加賀美氏の一族が進出した。
近世には[[甲州街道]]の[[韮崎宿]]、[[台ヶ原宿]]、[[教来石宿]]が整備された。また、韮崎宿から[[信濃国]]佐久地方へ至る[[佐久往還]]が整備され、若神子宿と長沢宿がおかれた。また[[富士川水運]]による廻米輸送も行われた。
[[1878年]]、[[郡区町村編制法]]により分割されることとなる。当初は西巨摩、東巨摩、南巨摩の3郡に分割する予定であったが、[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]の指令により、それぞれ[[北巨摩郡]]、[[中巨摩郡]]、[[南巨摩郡]]の3郡に分割された。
=== 近世以降の沿革 === : ''所属町村の変遷は[[南巨摩郡#郡発足までの沿革]]、[[中巨摩郡#郡発足までの沿革]]、[[北巨摩郡#郡発足までの沿革]]をそれぞれ参照'' * 「[[旧高旧領取調帳]]」に記載されている[[明治]]初年時点での支配は以下の通り。（341村） ** 後の'''南巨摩郡'''域（77村） - 市川代官所、[[田安徳川家]]領 ** 後の'''中巨摩郡'''域（153村） - 市川代官所、[[甲府代官所]]、田安徳川家領 ** 後の'''北巨摩郡'''域（111村） - 市川代官所、甲府代官所 * [[慶応]]4年 ** [[5月24日 (旧暦)|5月24日]]（[[1868年]][[7月13日]]） - 田安徳川家が立藩して'''[[田安徳川家|田安藩]]'''となる。 ** [[9月4日 (旧暦)|9月4日]]（1868年[[10月19日]]） - 甲府代官所・市川代官所の管轄地がそれぞれ'''[[府中県]]'''・'''[[市川県]]'''の管轄となる。 ** [[10月28日 (旧暦)|10月28日]]（1868年[[12月11日]]） - 田安藩領を除く全域が'''[[甲斐府]]'''の管轄となる。 * 明治2年 ** [[7月20日 (旧暦)|7月20日]]（[[1869年]][[8月27日]]） - 甲斐府が'''[[甲府県]]'''に改称。 ** [[12月26日 (旧暦)|12月26日]]（[[1870年]][[1月27日]]） - 田安藩が廃藩。 * 明治3年 ** 旧・田安藩領が正式に甲府県の管轄となる。 ** [[11月20日 (旧暦)|11月20日]]（[[1871年]][[1月10日]]） - 甲府県が'''[[山梨県]]'''に改称。 * 明治4年（1871年）から明治8年（[[1875年]]）にかけて町村の再編が行われる。（121村） * 明治11年（[[1878年]]）[[12月19日]] - [[郡区町村編制法]]の山梨県での施行により、鰍沢村ほか25村の区域に'''[[南巨摩郡]]'''、竜王村ほか52村の区域に'''[[中巨摩郡]]'''、河原部村ほか44村の区域に'''[[北巨摩郡]]'''がそれぞれ行政区画として発足。同日巨摩郡消滅。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|year=1984|date=1984-09-01|title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]]|publisher=[[角川書店]]|volume=19 山梨県|isbn=4040011902|ref={{SfnRef|角川日本地名大辞典|1984}}}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param 旧高旧領取調帳データベース] * [[原正人]]「巨麻郡と[[渡来人]]」『山梨県史通史編1原始・古代』第5章第2節

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[巨摩郡]] to [[Koma District]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{otheruses||漫画『バリバリ伝説』の登場人物|バリバリ伝説#第1部より登場}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|令制国一覧|東山道|甲斐国}} * {{Pathnav|日本|中部地方|山梨県}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|山梨県巨摩郡の範囲]] '''巨摩郡'''（こまぐん）は、[[山梨県]]（[[甲斐国]]）にあった[[郡]]。
== 郡域 == 消滅時の郡域は下記の区域にあたるが、行政区画として画定されたものではない。
* [[韮崎市]] * [[南アルプス市]] * [[北杜市]] * [[甲斐市]] * [[甲府市]]の一部（概ね[[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]以西および黒平町） * [[中央市]]の一部（[[笛吹川]]以北） * [[中巨摩郡]] * [[南巨摩郡]]の一部（[[富士川]]以西）
== 概要 == [[古代]]においては、'''巨麻郡'''とも表記され、中世以降に「巨摩」表記へ移行する。近世まで甲斐国は4郡で構成されていたが、その中で面積は最大。大まかにいうと甲斐国を東西に3分割した際の西部にあたる[[甲府盆地]]と南アルプス地域に相当する南北に長く広大な郡域で、のちの[[北巨摩郡]]、[[中巨摩郡]]の荒川以西、および[[南巨摩郡]]のうち富士川以西の地域に相当。
北部と北西部を[[信濃国]][[佐久郡]]、[[諏訪郡]]、[[伊那郡]]と接し、東は[[山梨郡]]、[[八代郡 (甲斐国)|八代郡]]と接し、西部と南部は[[駿河国]][[安倍郡]]、[[庵原郡]]と接する。北には[[八ヶ岳]]や[[茅ヶ岳]]、西には[[甲斐駒ヶ岳]]や[[北岳]]などがあり、郡中を釜無川が南流し、[[御勅使川]]や諸河川を合流して[[富士川]]にいたる。
=== 郡名の由来 === 初見史料は『[[正倉院文書]]』[[天平宝字]]5年（[[761年]]）12月「甲斐国司解」に見られる「巨麻郡栗原郷」。郡名の由来は二つの説があり、[[江戸時代]]に編纂された『[[甲斐国志]]』など近世の地誌類によれば、古代に多くの御[[牧 (古代)|牧]]が多く存在したことから、良馬（駒）を産出した地である事にちなみ「駒」という言葉が由来となったという説と、668年の[[朝鮮半島]]での[[高句麗]]滅亡以後、『[[続日本紀]]』霊亀2年（716年）5月16日条に甲斐はじめ関東や中部の7カ国へ高句麗からの[[渡来人|帰化人]]が渡っている記事が見られることから、[[高句麗]]からの帰化人にちなみ「高麗」という言葉が由来となったという説があった。[[近代]]までは前者の「駒説」が有力であったが、[[関晃]]による[[上代特殊仮名遣]]の研究により、「駒」のコは甲類、「高麗」のコは乙類で、乙類の「巨」に一致する「高麗説」が妥当であり、高句麗系渡来氏族大狛連（狛造）の本貫地である[[河内国]][[大県郡]]巨麻郷や[[若江郡]]巨麻郷に「巨麻」が当てられている事から、巨麻は高句麗（高麗）を意味すると考えられている<ref> {{Cite book|和書 |chapter=甲斐の帰化人 |title=甲斐史学 |volume=1号 |author=関晃|authorlink=関晃 |date=1957年 |year=1957 }}収録 - {{Cite book|和書 |title=関晃著作集 |volume=第5巻 |editor=関晃著作集編集委員会 |date=1997年2月 |year=1997 |publisher=[[吉川弘文館]] |id=ISBN 4-642-02305-4 }}</ref>。しかしながら「高」を甲類のコとする別の研究成果もあるため<ref> {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.techxel.jp/wp/wp-content/uploads/files/140107Manyogana.pdf |title=改訂新版・世界大百科事典「万葉仮名」の項（ジャパンナレッジ） |publisher=[[平凡社]]刊 |date=2007年 |year=2007 |accessdate=2018-10-13 }}</ref> <ref> {{Cite web|和書|url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=375 |title=万葉仮名甲類乙類音韻の研究 |author=[[田中健彦]]著 |date=2014年 |year=2014 |accessdate=2018-10-13 }}</ref>、万葉仮名の読みの分類から、巨麻と高麗と結びつける妥当性には諸説が存在する。
== 立評と古代の巨摩郡 == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|3頭分の馬歯が出土した甲斐市志田のお舟石古墳]] 『[[和名類聚抄]]』では等力、速見、栗原、青沼、真衣、大井、市川、川合、余部の9郷が見られる。このうち等力と栗原は[[飛び地]]であった可能性が指摘され、のちに青沼郷とともに山梨郡へ編入されている。また、市川郷の一部も八代郡へ編入される。古代甲斐国では[[甲斐の黒駒]]伝承に象徴される馬産が盛んな地域で、特に巨摩郡には[[真衣野牧]]・[[穂坂牧]]・[[柏前牧]]の三御牧が設置され、『[[延喜式]]』によれば朝廷に駒を貢進していたという。
巨麻郡へは[[朝鮮半島]]から[[高句麗]]遺民が遷置されたと考えられており、渡来人の墓制である[[積石塚]]が分布する。それ以前にも巨麻郡地域や甲斐国内へ定住していた[[渡来人]]集団の存在を示す形跡が残されている。甲府盆地の北縁では甲府市横根町・桜井町に分布する[[横根・桜井塚古墳群]]や、笛吹市春日居町の[[春日居古墳群]]など、[[甲府市]]羽黒町・湯村から横根町、笛吹市春日居町・石和町の山裾地域にかけて積石塚が分布している。中でも笛吹市石和町の[[大蔵経寺山古墳群]]の5世紀にまで遡る積石塚であると考えられている。
甲斐国と同じく積石塚が分布し、渡来人が立評に関わっているとされる[[信濃国]]北部の[[高井郡]]でも同様の傾向が見られる。高井郡でも古代には[[馬]]産が行われており、甲斐の巨摩郡でも積石塚の分布域と重なる盆地北縁の甲府市塩部の[[塩部遺跡]]や、盆地南部・[[曽根丘陵]]の甲府市下向山の[[東山北遺跡]]の[[方形周溝墓]]、[[甲斐市]][[志田 (甲斐市)|志田]]のお舟石古墳などで4世紀後半から5世紀初頭の[[馬歯]]や[[馬具]]が出土している<ref>村石「甲斐の馬生産の起源」『動物考古学』</ref>。信濃の高井郡や[[伊那谷]]も古墳時代からの馬の産地であり、甲斐・信濃の地域間での交流が指摘されている<ref>[[大塚初重]]「積石塚古墳と古代馬」『東国の古墳と大和政権』</ref>。
巨摩郡のうち積石塚が分布し古代において定住痕跡が見られる中核地域では、古代以前の[[縄文時代]]から[[弥生時代]]の遺跡が希薄な地域で、それまで未開発であった地域に対する立郡事情にも渡来人の関わりが考察されている。[[磯貝正義]]は栗原郷・等々力郷が飛び地であることに着目し、山梨郡の有力豪族が牧場経営などを目的に渡来人を利用して立郡したとする説を提唱している<ref>磯貝正義「郡の成立甲斐国巨麻郡の場合」『郡司及び采女制度の研究』</ref>。
一方、末木健は考古学的見地から、[[天狗沢瓦窯跡]]（甲斐市天狗沢）から出土した[[古代瓦]]に高句麗系軒丸瓦が発見されたことに着目し、寺院建立のために渡来系工人集団が畿内政権の支援を得て立郡した可能性を指摘している<ref>末木健「甲斐国巨麻郡の成立と展開」「甲斐仏教文化の成立」『山梨県立考古博物館・県埋蔵文化センター研究紀要』</ref>。
その後、巨摩郡における渡来人の足跡は途絶えるが、『[[続日本紀]]』に拠れば霊亀2年（[[716年]]）に甲斐を含む東国の高麗人を武蔵国へ移し高麗郡が置かれたとあり、中核集団が移住したとも考えられている。 <!-- === 式内社 === 『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]に記される郡内の[[式内社]]。 {{式内社一覧/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''巨摩郡''' |- ||||||||||||||| |} -->
== 中世以降 == 巨摩郡における[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]の初見は、『[[仁和寺文書]]』法勝院領目録の[[安和]]二年（[[969年]]）7月8日に記載される[[市河荘]]で、市河荘の立荘は甲斐国において早く、[[荘園公領制]]確立以前の10世紀に成立した荘園と評価されている。
中世には[[甘利荘]]、逸見、[[加々美荘]]、大井、[[布施荘]]、奈古、鎌田、大八幡、山小笠原、原小笠原、小井川、鷹津名、[[志摩荘]]などの諸庄が見られ、ほか小笠原、逸見、武川、八田、飯野などの牧や、藤井、杣などの[[保]]も成立する。平安時代後期には[[常陸国]]から流罪となった源義清・清光親子が市河荘に土着し、さらに八ヶ岳山麓の逸見牧をはじめその子孫は甲府盆地各地の荘園で進出し、[[甲斐源氏]]の一族となる。甲斐源氏は棟梁の[[武田氏|甲斐武田氏]]をはじめ、[[逸見氏]]、[[甲斐一条氏|一条氏]]、[[甘利氏]]、[[加賀美氏]]、[[小笠原氏]]、[[南部氏]]、[[奈古氏]]の氏族を分出した。このうち巨摩郡では棟梁の武田氏のほか、西郡一帯には加賀美氏の一族が進出した。
近世には[[甲州街道]]の[[韮崎宿]]、[[台ヶ原宿]]、[[教来石宿]]が整備された。また、韮崎宿から[[信濃国]]佐久地方へ至る[[佐久往還]]が整備され、若神子宿と長沢宿がおかれた。また[[富士川水運]]による廻米輸送も行われた。
[[1878年]]、[[郡区町村編制法]]により分割されることとなる。当初は西巨摩、東巨摩、南巨摩の3郡に分割する予定であったが、[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]の指令により、それぞれ[[北巨摩郡]]、[[中巨摩郡]]、[[南巨摩郡]]の3郡に分割された。
=== 近世以降の沿革 === : ''所属町村の変遷は[[南巨摩郡#郡発足までの沿革]]、[[中巨摩郡#郡発足までの沿革]]、[[北巨摩郡#郡発足までの沿革]]をそれぞれ参照'' * 「[[旧高旧領取調帳]]」に記載されている[[明治]]初年時点での支配は以下の通り。（341村） ** 後の'''南巨摩郡'''域（77村） - 市川代官所、[[田安徳川家]]領 ** 後の'''中巨摩郡'''域（153村） - 市川代官所、[[甲府代官所]]、田安徳川家領 ** 後の'''北巨摩郡'''域（111村） - 市川代官所、甲府代官所 * [[慶応]]4年 ** [[5月24日 (旧暦)|5月24日]]（[[1868年]][[7月13日]]） - 田安徳川家が立藩して'''[[田安徳川家|田安藩]]'''となる。 ** [[9月4日 (旧暦)|9月4日]]（1868年[[10月19日]]） - 甲府代官所・市川代官所の管轄地がそれぞれ'''[[府中県]]'''・'''[[市川県]]'''の管轄となる。 ** [[10月28日 (旧暦)|10月28日]]（1868年[[12月11日]]） - 田安藩領を除く全域が'''[[甲斐府]]'''の管轄となる。 * 明治2年 ** [[7月20日 (旧暦)|7月20日]]（[[1869年]][[8月27日]]） - 甲斐府が'''[[甲府県]]'''に改称。 ** [[12月26日 (旧暦)|12月26日]]（[[1870年]][[1月27日]]） - 田安藩が廃藩。 * 明治3年 ** 旧・田安藩領が正式に甲府県の管轄となる。 ** [[11月20日 (旧暦)|11月20日]]（[[1871年]][[1月10日]]） - 甲府県が'''[[山梨県]]'''に改称。 * 明治4年（1871年）から明治8年（[[1875年]]）にかけて町村の再編が行われる。（121村） * 明治11年（[[1878年]]）[[12月19日]] - [[郡区町村編制法]]の山梨県での施行により、鰍沢村ほか25村の区域に'''[[南巨摩郡]]'''、竜王村ほか52村の区域に'''[[中巨摩郡]]'''、河原部村ほか44村の区域に'''[[北巨摩郡]]'''がそれぞれ行政区画として発足。同日巨摩郡消滅。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}

To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).

== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=draft}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡|12=draft}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳|12=draft}}, and other peaks. 2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pp to pp]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saku District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe District|qq=}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihara District|qq=}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Yatsugatake|qq=}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kaikoma|qq=}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kitadake|qq=}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goguryeo|qq=}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wamyo Ruijusho|qq=}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Kaido|qq=}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of dissolved districts in Japan|qq=}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}
{{translated page|ja|巨摩郡|version=100203449|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Defunct districts of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kai Province districts]] [[Category:Minamikoma District]] [[Category:Nakakoma District]] [[Category:Kitakoma District]] [[Category:Koma District]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }}
[[sn:Koma District]] [[ja:巨摩郡]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Koma District]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saku District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe District|qq=}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihara District|qq=}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Yatsugatake|qq=}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kaikoma|qq=}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kitadake|qq=}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goguryeo|qq=}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wamyo Ruijusho|qq=}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Kaido|qq=}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of dissolved districts in Japan|qq=}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}
{{translated page|ja|巨摩郡|version=100203449|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Defunct districts of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kai Province districts]] [[Category:Minamikoma District]] [[Category:Nakakoma District]]
[[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]]<!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->[[sn:Koma District]][[Category:qq]] }}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District|ja|佐久郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saku District|qq=}}, {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa District|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District|ja|安倍郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe District|qq=}} and {{ill|Ihara District|ja|庵原郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihara District|qq=}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Mount Yatsugatake|ja|八ヶ岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Yatsugatake|qq=}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|ja|甲斐駒ヶ岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kaikoma|qq=}}, {{ill|Mount Kitadake|ja|北岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kitadake|qq=}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goguryeo|qq=}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wamyo Ruijusho|qq=}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Kaido|qq=}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of dissolved districts in Japan|qq=}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}

{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].

* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Defunct districts of Yamanashi Prefecture
revision.text:
{{otheruses||Character from the manga "Baribari Densetsu"|Baribari Densetsu#Characters introduced in Part 1}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}
{{translated page|ja|巨摩郡|version=100203449|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|qq=}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|qq=}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|qq=}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|qq=}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. **"Koma" (駒) Theory**: As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|qq=}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|qq=}}.
2. **"Koma" (高麗) Theory**: This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|qq=}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|qq=}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|qq=}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks.
