<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years= - [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Minamikoma District]]・[[Nakakoma District]]・[[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}

== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:巨摩郡]] {{wikidata link|Q11479803}} [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Districts of Kai Province]] [[Category:Former districts of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kitakoma District]] [[Category:Minamikoma District]] [[Category:Nakakoma District]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered [[:en:Saku District, Shinano|Saku District]], [[:en:Suwa District, Nagano|Suwa District]], and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

To the south and west, it bordered [[:en:Abe District, Shizuoka|Abe District]] and [[:en:Ihara District, Shizuoka|Ihara District]] in Suruga Province.
Significant features included [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Mount Yatsugatake]], [[:en:Mount Kaikoma|Mount Kaikoma]], [[:en:Mount Kita|Mount Kitadake]], and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]], Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11479803]] Wikidata name: Koma district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a {{ill|District|en|District|WD=Q149621|lt=District}} in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (formerly {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District, Shinano|en|Saku District, Shinano|WD=Q7403130|lt=Saku District}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District, Shizuoka|en|Abe District, Shizuoka|WD=Q4666382|lt=Abe District}} and {{ill|Ihara District, Shizuoka|en|Ihara District, Shizuoka|WD=Q988501|lt=Ihara District}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Mount Yatsugatake}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|en|Mount Kaikoma|WD=Q3695507|lt=Mount Kaikoma}}, {{ill|Mount Kita|en|Mount Kita|WD=Q961873|lt=Mount Kitadake}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Kōshū Kaidō|en|Kōshū Kaidō|WD=Q1156682|lt=Koshu Kaido}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]]
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a {{ill|District|en|District|WD=Q149621|lt=District}} in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (formerly {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District, Shinano|en|Saku District, Shinano|WD=Q7403130|lt=Saku District}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District, Shizuoka|en|Abe District, Shizuoka|WD=Q4666382|lt=Abe District}} and {{ill|Ihara District, Shizuoka|en|Ihara District, Shizuoka|WD=Q988501|lt=Ihara District}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Mount Yatsugatake}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|en|Mount Kaikoma|WD=Q3695507|lt=Mount Kaikoma}}, {{ill|Mount Kita|en|Mount Kita|WD=Q961873|lt=Mount Kitadake}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Kōshū Kaidō|en|Kōshū Kaidō|WD=Q1156682|lt=Koshu Kaido}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|en|List of dissolved districts in Japan|WD=Q6616953|lt=List of dissolved districts in Japan}}

According to the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Kōshū Kaidō|en|Kōshū Kaidō|WD=Q1156682|lt=Koshu Kaido}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:巨摩郡]][[zh:巨摩郡]]
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Koma-gun.png|frame|The extent of Koma District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Koma District''' (巨摩郡, こまぐん) was a {{ill|District|en|District|WD=Q149621|lt=District|1=District}} in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (formerly {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}}).
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* [[Nirasaki City]] * [[Minami-Alps City]] * [[Hokuto City]] * [[Kai City]] * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally west of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}} and including Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Chuo City]] (north of the Fuefuki River) * [[Nakakoma District]] * Part of [[Minamikoma District]] (west of the Fujikawa River)
== Overview == In ancient times, the district was also written as 巨麻郡, transitioning to the "Koma" (巨摩) spelling in the medieval period. It was the largest of the four districts in Kai Province. The district extended from the western portion of the [[Kōfu Basin|Kofu Basin]] to the Minami-Alps region, roughly corresponding to the areas later divided into [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and part of [[Minamikoma District]] west of the Fujikawa River.
To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District, Shinano|en|Saku District, Shinano|WD=Q7403130|lt=Saku District|1=Saku District, Shinano}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District|1=Suwa District, Nagano}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District, Shizuoka|en|Abe District, Shizuoka|WD=Q4666382|lt=Abe District|1=Abe District, Shizuoka}} and {{ill|Ihara District, Shizuoka|en|Ihara District, Shizuoka|WD=Q988501|lt=Ihara District|1=Ihara District, Shizuoka}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Mount Yatsugatake|1=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|en|Mount Kaikoma|WD=Q3695507|lt=Mount Kaikoma|1=Mount Kaikoma}}, {{ill|Mount Kita|en|Mount Kita|WD=Q961873|lt=Mount Kitadake|1=Mount Kita}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Kōshū Kaidō|en|Kōshū Kaidō|WD=Q1156682|lt=Koshu Kaido|1=Kōshū Kaidō}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|en|List of dissolved districts in Japan|WD=Q6616953|lt=List of dissolved districts in Japan|1=List of dissolved districts in Japan}}

To the north and northwest, Koma District bordered {{ill|Saku District, Shinano|en|Saku District, Shinano|WD=Q7403130|lt=Saku District|1=Saku District, Shinano}}, {{ill|Suwa District, Nagano|en|Suwa District, Nagano|WD=Q1131473|lt=Suwa District|1=Suwa District, Nagano}}, and {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} in Shinano Province. To the east, it bordered {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi District|qq=}} and {{ill|Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|ja|八代郡 (甲斐国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro District (Kai Province)|1=Yatsushiro district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390569}}. To the south and west, it bordered {{ill|Abe District, Shizuoka|en|Abe District, Shizuoka|WD=Q4666382|lt=Abe District|1=Abe District, Shizuoka}} and {{ill|Ihara District, Shizuoka|en|Ihara District, Shizuoka|WD=Q988501|lt=Ihara District|1=Ihara District, Shizuoka}} in Suruga Province.
Significant features included {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Mount Yatsugatake|1=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}}, {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|en|Mount Kaikoma|WD=Q3695507|lt=Mount Kaikoma|1=Mount Kaikoma}}, {{ill|Mount Kita|en|Mount Kita|WD=Q961873|lt=Mount Kitadake|1=Mount Kita}}, and other peaks. The Kamanashi River flowed southward through the district, merging with tributaries like the {{ill|Miyama River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyama River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} before joining the Fujikawa River.
=== Origin of the District Name === The earliest record of Koma District appears in the {{ill|Shosoin Documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosoin Documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, dated to Tenpyo-hoji 5 (761), which mentions "Kuriwara Subdistrict in Koma District, Kai Province." Two main theories explain the origin of the name:
1. '''"Koma" (駒) Theory''': As suggested in Edo-period works such as the {{ill|Kai Koku-shi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Koku-shi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}}, the name derives from the district's historical production of fine horses (駒) in its many {{ill|Imperial Horse Pastures|ja|牧 (古代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Horse Pastures|1=maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60986436}}.
2. '''"Koma" (高麗) Theory''': This theory links the name to {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} immigrants who settled in the district after the fall of Goguryeo in 668. The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} records that immigrants were relocated to eastern and central Japan in 716, suggesting that the district name reflects its status as a settlement area for these people.
Research by Akira Seki supports the latter theory, associating the kanji 巨摩 with "Koma" (高麗), the Japanese term for Goguryeo.
== Establishment and Ancient History == [[File:Ofuneishi Kofun Shida Kai City.jpg|thumb|250px|The Ofuneishi Kofun in Kai City, where horse teeth were unearthed]] According to the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}, Koma District contained nine subdistricts: Toriki, Hayami, Kuriwara, Aonuma, Makomo, Oi, Ichikawa, Kawai, and Amarube. Among these, Toriki and Kuriwara may have been exclaves and were later incorporated into Yamanashi District. Ichikawa was partially incorporated into Yatsushiro District.
Koma District was a center of horse production, with three notable imperial pastures: {{ill|Maenuno-no-Makibe|ja|真衣野牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maenuno-no-Makibe|1=makinonomaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230325}}, {{ill|Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja|穂坂牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosaka-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja|柏前牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwano-no-Makibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, these pastures supplied horses to the imperial court.
Archaeological evidence, such as {{ill|Sekishizuka|ja|積石塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekishizuka|1=stone mound tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597152}} tombs and kofun, reflects the influence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula, who contributed to the district's establishment and early development.
== Medieval and Early Modern Periods == During the medieval period, the district saw the establishment of manors such as {{ill|Ichikawa Manor|ja|市河荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa Manor|1=Ichikawashō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480614}}, {{ill|Kamata Manor|ja|鎌田荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamata Manor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Fuse Manor|ja|布施荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuse Manor|1=fusenoshou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480832}}, as well as settlements like Makibetsu and Komi. The {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|甲斐源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, including prominent families like the Takeda, Iwami, Ichijo, and Naito, emerged from this region.
In the Edo period, transportation infrastructure such as the {{ill|Kōshū Kaidō|en|Kōshū Kaidō|WD=Q1156682|lt=Koshu Kaido|1=Kōshū Kaidō}}, Nirasaki-juku, and Fuji River shipping routes supported economic development.
== Meiji Restoration and Beyond == In 1878, the district was divided into three administrative regions: [[Minamikoma District]], [[Nakakoma District]], and [[:en:Kitakoma District|Kitakoma District]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|en|List of dissolved districts in Japan|WD=Q6616953|lt=List of dissolved districts in Japan|1=List of dissolved districts in Japan}}
